title: Why Transitions Between Activities Are So Activating
researcherTopic: Internal Family Systems, parts work, Self-energy
metaDescription: Discover why simple transitions between activities can feel so activating for those with complex trauma (C-PTSD). Learn how your nervous system's wiring and past experiences contribute to this common survival response.
imageDescription: A warm, luminous image of golden light filtering through a window, illuminating the delicate, intricate web of a spider plant. The focus is on the interplay of light and shadow, suggesting the complex and beautiful inner workings of the nervous system. The overall feeling is one of peace, resilience, and gentle growth.
htmlContent:
What happens when the body remembers something the mind has spent decades trying to forget? For those of us who have navigated the complex territory of developmental trauma, the answer often lies in the surprisingly fraught moments of transition ~ the simple, seemingly innocuous shifts from one state of being to another. For a nervous system wired for survival, these gaps are not empty; they are potent, charged with the echoes of past experiences, and they can become significantly activating in ways that defy simple explanation. We are not reacting to the present moment’s transition, but to the accumulated weight of a thousand unsafe transitions that came before.

The Interruption of Flow as a Threat Signal

For a nervous system that has found a precarious sense of equilibrium, any change can feel like a threat. We might find ourselves in a state of relative calm or focus ~ a state we’ve worked hard to achieve... and the interruption of that state, the requirement to shift our attention and energy, can feel like a striking violation. It’s not the new activity itself that is the problem, but the breaking of the fragile peace we had established. This isn’t a conscious choice, but a deeply ingrained, somatic response. It is a return to a state of hypervigilance, a state that is exhausting and unsustainable, yet feels like the only way to survive. We might find ourselves resisting the transition, procrastinating, or feeling a surge of anger or anxiety that seems disproportionate to the situation. This is the body’s wisdom, distorted by trauma, trying to protect us from a danger that is no longer present but feels imminently real. We are not lazy or undisciplined; we are simply trying to manage a nervous system that is doing exactly what it was designed to do in a world that no longer exists.

The Body's Grammar of Unsafety

Complex trauma teaches the body a language of unsafety, a grammar of activation that it never forgets. Every cell holds the memory of what it felt like to be unsafe, and transitions can act as a powerful trigger for these somatic memories. It’s not a cognitive process; it’s a felt sense. The shift from a state of rest to a state of activity, or vice versa, can be enough to bring the body back to a time when such shifts were perilous. Perhaps it was the sound of a parent’s footsteps approaching, the sudden silence that preceded an outburst, or the chaotic energy of a household in turmoil. The body remembers the feeling of dread, the tightening in the chest, the shallowing of the breath. And so, in the present moment, as we move from one activity to another, the body can react as if the original trauma is happening all over again. His model of Internal Family Systems (IFS) helps us understand that we are not a single, monolithic self, but a collection of ‘parts,’ each with its own history, beliefs, and burdens. When we experience a difficult transition, it is often because one of our parts... a young, wounded part... has been activated. This part doesn’t know that we are now an adult, that we are safe, that the danger has passed. It only knows the terror of the past, and it is trying to protect us in the only way it knows how. By learning to listen to these parts, to understand their stories, and to offer them the compassion and care they never received, we can begin to heal the deep wounds of the past.

The Cognitive Cost of Shifting Gears

The Echo of Unmet Needs

The Nervous System's Predictive Power

Wild, right?

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link).

The Space Between: A Territory of Embodied Awareness

While the challenges of transitions are real and deep, they also offer us a powerful opportunity for healing. The space between activities, the pause, the gap... this is the territory of embodied awareness. It is a place where we can begin to notice, without judgment, what is happening in our bodies and minds. It is a place where we can begin to cultivate a different relationship with our experience, one that is based on curiosity and compassion rather than fear and resistance. Instead of trying to push through the discomfort of a transition, what if we were to simply pause and notice it? What if we were to bring a gentle, curious attention to the sensations in our bodies, to the thoughts in our minds, to the emotions that are arising? It is about learning to tolerate the discomfort, to stay present with the activation, to ride the waves of our experience without being swept away by them. In my years of working in this territory, I’ve sat with people who describe this as a striking shift in their relationship with themselves. It is a move from a state of constant struggle to a state of gentle, compassionate presence. It is a move from a life lived in reaction to a life lived in response. And it is in this space, this territory of embodied awareness, that the real work of healing begins. For those interested in exploring this further, our article on the practice of somatic mindfulness offers a deeper dive.

Cultivating a New Relationship with Transitions

Healing from the activating nature of transitions is not about eliminating the activation itself, but about changing our relationship to it. It is about learning to meet the activation with a sense of curiosity and compassion, rather than with fear and resistance. It is about understanding that the activation is not a sign that we are broken, but a sign that our nervous system is trying to protect us. And it is about learning to provide our nervous system with the safety and support it needs to finally, after all these years, stand down. That becomes not a quick or easy process. But it is possible. It is possible to learn to navigate the spaces between with a sense of ease and grace. It is possible to transform these moments of activation into moments of connection and healing. And it is possible to come to a place where transitions are no longer a source of dread, but an opportunity for a deeper, more intimate relationship with ourselves. This is the promise of the healing process - not a life without pain, but a life in which we are no longer defined by it.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why do I feel so exhausted after a simple transition?

What we find here is a common experience for those with C-PTSD. The exhaustion you feel is not just mental, but physical. Your nervous system is expending a tremendous amount of energy to manage the perceived threat of the transition. Be kind to yourself in these moments. Rest when you need to. And know that the exhaustion is a sign of how hard your body is working to keep you safe.

A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link).

How can I support myself during a difficult transition?

One of the most powerful things you can do is to simply pause. Before you move from one activity to another, take a few moments to check in with yourself. Notice the sensations in your body, the thoughts in your mind, the emotions that are present. And then, when you feel ready, move into the next activity with a sense of gentle, compassionate awareness. This simple act of pausing can make a world of difference.

Will I ever be able to handle transitions without getting activated?

Healing is not about getting rid of the activation, but about changing your relationship to it. Over time, as you continue to do the work of healing, you will likely find that the activation becomes less intense and less frequent. And you will come to a place where you can navigate transitions with a sense of greater ease and confidence. But the goal is not to eliminate the activation, but to meet it with wisdom and compassion.

What if I can't feel anything in my body during transitions?

Numbness and dissociation are common responses to trauma. If you can’t feel anything in your body, that’s okay. Don’t try to force it. Simply notice the numbness. Over time, as your nervous system begins to feel safer, you may find that sensation begins to return. But for now, simply being with the numbness is enough. It is a courageous act of presence.

A discreet grounding tool you can stick anywhere is Calm Strips Sensory Stickers (paid link).

Not what you expected, right?

In our work at Kalesh.love, we explore the path of healing through the lens of embodied consciousness, offering a way to come home to yourself. For those seeking further information from a clinical perspective, the National Institute of Mental Health and the National Center for Biotechnology Information provide a wealth of research and resources on the impacts of trauma.

What we are ultimately seeking is not a life without difficulty, but a life in which we have the capacity to meet our difficulties with an open and compassionate heart. It is a work of returning to ourselves, of reclaiming the parts of ourselves that we have lost, of coming home to the truth of who we are. And in the tender space of our own hearts, we find that we have everything we need to heal.

A breathing tool that helps slow the exhale for nervous system regulation is Breathing Necklace - Shift (paid link).

Are you willing to be uncomfortable long enough for something to shift?
